Vitamin D Status in Ghana: A Mixed-Methods Narrative Review of Prevalence, Determinants,
Comorbidity Burden, and Spatial Distribution with Machine Learning Risk Prediction

Valentine Golden Ghanem, MSc
Ghana COCOBOD Cocoa Clinic, Accra, Ghana | ORCID: 0009-0002-8332-0220 | valentineghanem@gmail.com

Background

Vitamin D deficiency (VDD; 25(OH)D <20 ng/mL) affects approximately 1 billion people globally, yet paradoxically burdens equatorial populations despite year-round UVB exposure. Ghana (4–11°N) provides a natural laboratory to investigate this paradox. No comprehensive synthesis of Ghanaian vitamin D evidence existed prior to this review.

20
Studies Included
6,109
Total Participants
41.3%
Pooled VDD Prevalence
21.6 ng/mL
Weighted Mean 25(OH)D

Methods

Design: Mixed-methods narrative review (PRISMA-ScR)

Search: PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science (inception–March 2025); 90 records, 52 after deduplication, 20 included

Analysis:

  • Weighted pooled prevalence (inverse-variance weighting)
  • Spatial: Global Moran’s I (KNN k=4), Getis-Ord Gi*, latitude/longitude correlations
  • ML: Random Forest, XGBoost, Logistic Regression (10-fold stratified CV, 100 bootstrap iterations)
  • Feature importance: Permutation-based (30 repeats)

Quality: Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (all studies scored 6–8/9)

Conclusions

VDD affects 41.3% of studied Ghanaian populations, with >60% prevalence among pregnant women and T2DM patients. All published evidence is concentrated in 3 of 16 regions—a critical research gap requiring:

  • Nationally representative vitamin D surveys covering all 16 regions
  • Routine antenatal and metabolic clinic VDD screening
  • LC-MS/MS assay standardisation (VDSP participation)
  • Targeted research funding for 13 underrepresented regions

Results: Geographic Distribution

Figure 1. Geographic distribution across Ghana’s 16 administrative regions. Data available for only 3 regions.

Results: Regional Variation

Figure 2. Weighted VDD prevalence. Ashanti: 56.4%, Eastern: 33.2%, Greater Accra: 30.0%. Grey = no data.

Results: Spatial Analysis

MetricValueInterpretation
Global Moran’s I0.057 (p=0.058)No significant clustering
Latitude–VDDr=0.500 (p=0.025)Higher lat → higher VDD
Longitude–VDDr=−0.555 (p=0.011)Further west → higher VDD
Coverage3/16 regions (18.8%)Critical gap

BYM/INLA not feasible with 3-region coverage.

Results: ML Risk Prediction

ModelAUCAccuracySens.Spec.
Random Forest0.7020.6380.5220.738
XGBoost0.6870.6290.5100.731
Logistic Regression0.6960.6410.5780.695

Top predictors: Age (0.029), Publication year (0.023), Study design (0.011), Geographic region (0.010)

Policy Implications

  • Screening: Routine VDD screening at antenatal and metabolic clinics
  • Supplementation: Evidence-based guidelines for populations with >50% VDD
  • Research equity: Targeted funding for 13 unstudied regions
  • Standardisation: Transition to LC-MS/MS or VDSP participation
